headline: US and Iran reignite their war over the Strait of Hormuz: Trump drops a 20% shipping fee but presses a naval blockade as strikes fly, pushing oil to a one-month high.
the_big_picture: The single thread running through today is the re-escalation of the US-Iran conflict at the Strait of Hormuz, the chokepoint that carries roughly a fifth of the world's oil. Trump backed off a threatened 20% transit fee but is pushing ahead with a naval blockade while both sides trade strikes and tankers come under attack, sending crude to a one-month high. That oil spike is the hinge for everything downstream: US inflation cooled to 3.5% but officials flagged fresh upside risk, India's inflation just breached its central bank's target, the rupee slid past 96 and the Sensex fell. Running underneath, an AI storyline cuts both ways: IBM shed roughly $70bn in a day after stumbling on AI deals, even as China's AI-driven exports jumped 27% and Nvidia began shipping powerful H200 chips into China.

stories.title: Poll: Ukrainians wanted the general gone, not the tech minister — Fedorov's trust rating surges past Zelensky's amid daily protests
key_points: Poll: Fedorov's trust rating jumped from 35% to 65% in a week
72% of Ukrainians opposed his dismissal, just 5% backed it
Thousands protest nationwide daily, demanding his reappointment
Army chief Syrskyi's trust fell from 39% to 23% over same period
stories.background: Mykhailo Fedorov built Ukraine's wartime drone and tech program from scratch and became one of the most visible, popular faces of the war effort. Yesterday's coverage described Zelensky firing both Fedorov and army chief Syrskyi within the same week and installing a younger war cabinet, reportedly to resolve a personal clash between the two men.
stories.what_happened: New polling from Rating Group, fielded July 20-21, shows Ukrainians' trust in Fedorov jumped from 35% to 65% in a single week — now higher than trust in Zelensky himself — while trust in Syrskyi fell from 39% to 23% over the same period. Separately, 72% of respondents said they did not support Fedorov's dismissal, versus just 5% who agreed with it. Thousands have rallied in Kyiv and other cities daily since the firing, demanding his reappointment.
stories.why_it_matters: A president firing a genuinely popular official to settle an internal power struggle risks turning a private management problem into a public trust problem — especially in wartime, when unity and morale matter as much as any battlefield decision. The poll suggests Zelensky read the situation differently than the public did: people wanted Syrskyi gone, not Fedorov.
stories.ripple_effects: Sustained street protests during an active war put pressure on Zelensky's government at a moment it can least afford internal distraction, and could shape how allies read Ukraine's political stability heading into any future ceasefire negotiations. It may also push Zelensky toward finding Fedorov a face-saving way back into government.
stories.why_now: The poll numbers and the protests are compounding each other — each new rally amplifies the polling story, and each new poll gives protesters a stronger case that the dismissal was a political misstep, not a routine personnel decision.
stories.watch_next: Whether Zelensky reverses course and offers Fedorov some form of reinstatement, or whether the protests fade as the new war cabinet beds in.

key_terms.definition: When a government replaces or moves around ministers in its top team, often to fix internal problems or respond to public pressure.

stories.the_lesson: Firing a popular official to resolve an internal power struggle can backfire — a leader who removes someone the public trusts more than they trust the leader risks turning a management problem into a public-approval crisis.

thread.previously: Zelensky fired both defense minister Fedorov and army chief Syrskyi within the same week and installed a younger war cabinet, reportedly over a personal rift between the two men.
thread.changed: New polling shows the public wanted the opposite outcome — Fedorov's trust rating surged to 65% (above Zelensky's own), 72% opposed his dismissal, and thousands are protesting nationwide demanding he be reinstated.

stories.title: Russia bombs Black Sea grain ships; Ukraine calls emergency UN meeting as the corridor goes to zero traffic
key_points: Russia bombed 3+ civilian cargo ships in the Black Sea this week
10 crew killed when missiles hit grain ship Golden Leo
Zero ships crossed Ukraine's grain corridor Wednesday — peak harvest season
Ukraine calls emergency UN Security Council meeting for July 27
stories.background: Since Russia's 2022 invasion, Ukraine has depended on a negotiated 'safe corridor' through the Black Sea to keep exporting grain, sunflower oil and other food that much of Africa, the Middle East and Asia rely on. Russia has repeatedly threatened or attacked that corridor when talks stall, and this spike in ship strikes lands just as the separate US-brokered Iran ceasefire is also collapsing — straining global shipping and insurance markets on two fronts at once.
stories.what_happened: Russia has bombed at least three civilian cargo ships in the Black Sea in the past few days, including a Kh-59/Kh-69 cruise-missile strike on the cargo ship Golden Leo on July 19 that killed 10 crew as it left Ukraine's grain corridor loaded with grain. Ukraine's acting foreign minister Andrii Sybiha said Wednesday that zero vessels passed through the corridor that day — right at the peak of the summer harvest — and called for an emergency UN Security Council meeting, now set for July 27.
stories.why_it_matters: Ukraine is one of the world's largest wheat and sunflower-oil exporters, and countries in Africa, the Middle East and South Asia depend on that grain arriving cheaply and on time. A shipping corridor that goes to zero traffic, even briefly, can push up food prices for the world's poorest importers long before it shows up as a headline economic number anywhere else.
stories.ripple_effects: Grain prices and shipping-insurance costs typically rise on this kind of news, hitting countries like Egypt that import a large share of their wheat from the Black Sea region. It also raises the temperature at the UN, where Russia holds a Security Council veto — meaning the 'emergency meeting' Ukraine called for is likely to produce statements and pressure, not a binding resolution.
stories.why_now: The attacks intensified just as ceasefire talks over the separate Iran-Israel-US conflict are also falling apart, and Sybiha explicitly compared Russia's Black Sea campaign to Iran's threats against the Strait of Hormuz — suggesting both wars are converging on the same tactic: choking a chokepoint to raise the economic cost of the war for everyone else.
stories.watch_next: The emergency UN Security Council session on July 27, and whether shipping traffic through the corridor resumes before then.

key_terms.definition: A safe shipping lane Ukraine set up after Russia's invasion so cargo ships could keep exporting grain and other food through the Black Sea despite the war.

The United Nations' top body for peace and security. Its five permanent members (US, Russia, China, UK, France) can each veto any resolution, which is why 'emergency meetings' often produce statements rather than binding action.

stories.the_lesson: When a war disrupts a narrow shipping corridor, the damage isn't just local — it can ripple into food prices and shortages on the other side of the world, because global supply chains route an outsized share of a staple crop through a few chokepoints.
stories.market_link: Wheat and other grain futures, shipping and marine-insurance costs, and food-import-dependent economies (Egypt, parts of Africa and South Asia) are the most exposed if the corridor stays shut.

stories.title: Oil jumps toward $95 a barrel as the US-Iran ceasefire fully collapses and Rubio calls Iran 'not serious' about peace
key_points: Brent crude rose as much as 4.9% toward $95/barrel, a nearly six-week high
US strikes hit Iran for an 11th straight night as diplomacy stalls
Rubio says Tehran is 'not serious' about talks — a sharp reversal from his stance a day earlier
US pump prices are climbing again, extending their rise above $4/gallon
stories.background: The US and Iran agreed a ceasefire in April after a direct war, and signed a memorandum of understanding in June meant to lead to a lasting peace. That truce has repeatedly broken down since, with both sides trading strikes — the pattern our earlier coverage has tracked through Iran's tanker attacks and Trump's retaliation threats.
stories.what_happened: US Central Command carried out an 11th consecutive night of strikes on Iran this week, as Secretary of State Marco Rubio declared Tehran 'not serious' about diplomatic talks — a notable hardening from his comments just a day earlier that the US remained open to a deal. Brent crude jumped nearly 5% toward $95 a barrel and US pump prices extended their climb above $4 a gallon as traders priced in a ceasefire that looks fully collapsed rather than merely strained.
stories.why_it_matters: This isn't just a Middle East story — it's now a direct hit to household budgets everywhere, showing up in gas prices within days. The shift in US rhetoric from 'open to a deal' to 'not serious' suggests Washington sees less near-term chance of de-escalation, which markets are treating as a signal to keep pricing in disruption risk.
stories.ripple_effects: Higher oil flows through to diesel, jet fuel, and shipping costs, adding to inflation pressure just as central banks were hoping price growth would keep cooling. Airlines, freight companies, and any business reliant on fuel costs face squeezed margins, and emerging markets that import oil (including India) face pressure on their currencies and trade balances.
stories.why_now: Rubio's blunter language and the 11th straight night of strikes mark a real escalation from where the story stood just a day ago, when he still described the US as open to a deal.
stories.watch_next: Whether oil breaks decisively above $95 if strikes continue, and whether any third party (the UN, Gulf states) steps in to broker a new truce attempt.

key_terms.definition: The global benchmark price for oil, based on crude pumped from the North Sea — used to price roughly two-thirds of the world's traded oil.

stories.the_lesson: Geopolitical conflict reaches ordinary people fastest through energy prices — a ceasefire's health can be read almost in real time at the gas pump, long before the news fully explains why.
stories.market_link: Oil majors and airlines/shippers with fuel exposure move on this; watch WTI/Brent, jet-fuel-heavy carriers, and inflation-sensitive rate-cut bets.

thread.previously: We reported Rubio saying the US remained open to an Iran deal, while warning that Iran's demand over a Hormuz shipping toll could 'threaten the world economy.'
thread.changed: Rubio has now hardened his tone, calling Iran 'not serious' about talks, as US strikes reach an 11th straight night and oil jumps toward $95 a barrel — the ceasefire looks fully collapsed rather than just strained.

stories.title: Zelenskyy fires Ukraine's top general and defense minister in one week, installs a younger war cabinet
key_points: Zelenskyy sacked army chief Oleksandr Syrskyi, days after removing defense minister Mykhailo Fedorov
43-year-old Mykhailo Drapatyi named the new commander-in-chief
Move followed a week of rare wartime protests in Kyiv over war strategy
Drapatyi has pledged to 'intensify offensive operations in all domains'
stories.background: Ukraine has fought Russia's full-scale invasion since February 2022. Zelenskyy had largely kept the same senior military and political team in place for most of the war, valuing continuity. Syrskyi, a Soviet-trained general nicknamed 'the Butcher' by critics for costly battlefield decisions, had led Ukraine's armed forces since February 2024. Frustration over his strategy — and over the war effort's broader direction — had been building for months among soldiers and the public.
stories.what_happened: President Volodymyr Zelenskyy fired commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi on Wednesday and replaced him with 43-year-old General Mykhailo Drapatyi, described as part of a newer generation of commanders. The move came days after Zelenskyy removed defense minister and close ally Mykhailo Fedorov, whose public clashes with Syrskyi over military strategy triggered rare street protests in Kyiv. Drapatyi said the military would 'continue and intensify offensive operations in all domains.'
stories.why_it_matters: This is the biggest shake-up of Ukraine's wartime leadership in years, and it happened because ordinary Ukrainians took to the streets — an unusual show of public pressure in a country at war. It signals Zelenskyy will overhaul his inner circle rather than ride out discontent, and puts a younger, war-forged generation of commanders in charge of strategy against Russia.
stories.ripple_effects: A change in military leadership can shift battlefield tactics and morale quickly — allies like the US and European NATO members will watch closely to see if Drapatyi's promised 'intensified' operations mean a more aggressive posture, which could affect the pace of any future ceasefire talks with Russia. It also tests Zelenskyy's authority at a moment when Western military aid and domestic patience are both being tested by a grinding war now in its fifth year.
stories.why_now: A week of protests over Syrskyi's leadership — set off by his rift with the now-fired defense minister — forced Zelenskyy's hand faster than a typical reshuffle would.
stories.watch_next: Whether Drapatyi's 'intensify' pledge shows up as a visible change in Ukraine's battlefield tactics in the coming weeks, and how Russia responds.

key_terms.definition: The top uniformed officer running a country's military operations — distinct from the president, who is the civilian head of state and nominal supreme commander.

stories.the_lesson: In a democracy fighting a long war, sustained public pressure can force a leader to change course even mid-conflict — wartime unity doesn't mean immunity from accountability.

stories.title: A US judge sets a June 2027 trial date for Venezuela's ousted leader Maduro
key_points: Judge sets June 1, 2027 as the trial date for Maduro and wife Cilia Flores
Both face US drug-trafficking, kidnapping and murder charges
Maduro was captured by US special forces in Caracas in January
Defense will argue in November that Maduro is immune from US prosecution
stories.background: In January, US special forces carried out an overnight raid on Nicolás Maduro's home in Caracas and flew him and his wife, Cilia Flores, to the United States, ending his rule of Venezuela. Since then the country has been run by his former vice president, Delcy Rodríguez, who has built closer ties with the Trump administration. US federal prosecutors indicted Maduro and Flores in New York, accusing them of conspiring with drug cartels to move thousands of tons of cocaine into the US. Both pleaded not guilty and, through their lawyers, have disputed the very legitimacy of trying a former head of state in a US court.
stories.what_happened: At a hearing in Manhattan federal court on Wednesday, Judge Alvin Hellerstein set June 1, 2027 as the trial date for Maduro and Flores. He also scheduled a separate hearing for November on a defense motion to dismiss the case on grounds of sovereign immunity — the argument that Maduro cannot be prosecuted in a US court because he was acting as the head of a sovereign nation.
stories.why_it_matters: This confirms the case is moving forward as a real, scheduled prosecution rather than stalling out, and sets up a fight over an unusual legal question: can the United States put a foreign head of state on criminal trial after removing him from power by force? How that plays out could shape how far US courts are willing to go against foreign leaders in the future.
stories.ripple_effects: It keeps Venezuela's political transition under Rodríguez unsettled for years to come, since Maduro's legal fate remains unresolved. Other governments — friendly and hostile to Washington alike — are watching closely, since a successful sovereign-immunity challenge (or its failure) would set a precedent other countries could invoke, or fear, in future disputes with the US.
stories.why_now: This was simply the next scheduling hearing in an already-running case, coming about six months after Maduro's capture and indictment.
stories.watch_next: The November hearing on the sovereign-immunity motion to dismiss — if the judge grants it, the case could collapse before ever reaching the June 2027 trial date.

key_terms.definition: A legal principle that shields a country's leaders from being sued or prosecuted in another country's courts for actions taken in their official capacity as head of state.

stories.the_lesson: Physically removing a foreign leader from power doesn't automatically settle the legal question of whether you can put him on trial — capturing someone and successfully prosecuting him in your own courts are two very different fights.

stories.title: Houthis strike two Saudi oil tankers, leaving one ablaze in the Red Sea
key_points: Houthis fired missiles and drones at Saudi-flagged tankers Encelia and Layla
Saudi state media confirms a fire on Encelia's bow; all crew safe
Houthis say the ships broke a blockade they declared against Saudi Arabia on Monday
First real attacks under that new blockade — a fresh front beyond their old campaign
stories.background: Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthi movement has attacked shipping in the Red Sea since 2023, originally targeting vessels it linked to Israel or its allies over the Gaza war. This week that campaign took a new turn: on Monday the Houthis declared a naval blockade specifically against Saudi Arabia, in retaliation for a Saudi blockade on Yemen and a strike on Sanaa's international airport. Today's tanker attacks are the first strikes carried out under that new, Saudi-specific blockade — separate from, and in addition to, the Houthis' long-running campaign against Israel-linked ships.
stories.what_happened: The Houthis said they hit two Saudi Arabian oil tankers, the Encelia and the Layla, with a mix of ballistic and cruise missiles and drones as they sailed the Red Sea about 70 nautical miles southwest of Saudi Arabia's Al Shuqaiq. Saudi Arabia's state news agency confirmed a fire broke out on the Encelia's bow after it was struck, but said all crew members were safe and the vessel was not in danger of sinking.
stories.why_it_matters: This is a real escalation, not just a threat: the Houthis are now directly hitting Saudi-flagged ships rather than only vessels linked to Israel or the US. That drags Saudi Arabia — which has tried to stay out of direct fighting — closer to becoming an active target in the wider Iran-linked regional conflict.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect insurance costs for ships sailing the Red Sea and Bab al-Mandeb Strait to climb further, adding to the freight and fuel cost pressures already hitting global trade. It also raises the odds of a direct Saudi military response, which would open yet another front in a region already stretched by the Iran-Israel-US conflict. Oil and gold prices, already elevated on Middle East risk (Brent near $96 a barrel, gold above $4,100 an ounce), have further reason to stay high.
stories.why_now: The attacks are the first follow-through on the blockade the Houthis announced just three days ago, timed to their claim that Saudi Arabia is blockading Yemen and struck Sanaa's airport.
stories.watch_next: Whether Saudi Arabia responds militarily, and whether the Houthis strike more tankers under the new blockade in the coming days.

key_terms.definition: A Yemeni armed movement, aligned with Iran, that controls much of northern Yemen and has fought a long civil war against the Saudi-backed Yemeni government.
A military operation to seal off a country's ports or shipping lanes, cutting off the flow of goods in or out.

stories.the_lesson: In a regional conflict, combatants often widen their target list to pressure new parties into the fight — turning what looked like a two-sided war into a multi-front crisis that's much harder to de-escalate.
stories.market_link: Adds to the Middle East risk premium already pushing Brent crude near $96/barrel and gold above $4,100/oz; Red Sea shipping insurance costs likely rise further.

thread.previously: Yesterday the Houthis announced they were imposing a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia in retaliation for a Saudi blockade on Yemen and an attack on Sanaa's airport — at that point it was a declaration, with no attacks yet reported.
thread.changed: Today the Houthis acted on that declaration for the first time, striking two named Saudi oil tankers and setting one on fire — moving the blockade from threat to reality.

stories.title: The US and China will hold their first formal AI talks in September — trying to jointly manage frontier-model risk instead of just restricting each other
key_points: First-ever dedicated US-China AI dialogue, planned for September
Grew out of the Trump-Xi summit held in May 2026
Timed ahead of Xi's planned US visit on September 24
Topics: powerful 'frontier' AI models, open-source model risks, IP theft
stories.background: For the past few years, the US and China have mostly dealt with each other's AI industries through one-sided tools: US export bans on advanced chips, and Chinese restrictions on rare materials the US tech industry needs. There has been no direct, dedicated channel where the two governments sit down specifically to discuss the risks of powerful AI systems — until now. The two countries' most powerful AI labs (American firms like Anthropic and OpenAI, Chinese firms like Moonshot AI) are racing to build ever more capable models, and both governments are increasingly worried about what happens if that race outpaces anyone's ability to control the technology.
stories.what_happened: Reuters sources report the US and China have agreed to hold their first formal, AI-specific talks in September 2026, led on the US side by Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent. The meeting follows the Trump-Xi summit in May and is expected to happen before Xi's planned visit to the US on September 24. The agenda and venue are still being finalised, but the focus will be on 'frontier' AI models — the most advanced systems either country builds — including how each side controls release of future models and whether the US will restrict Chinese open-weight (freely downloadable) models.
stories.why_it_matters: This is a real shift in posture: instead of each government unilaterally restricting the other (export bans, tariffs, blacklists), they're proposing to sit down and jointly manage a risk both now admit they share — that an AI model released by either country could cause harm neither side can contain alone. It's the clearest sign yet that AI has become a top-tier diplomatic issue, on par with nuclear arms control in how it's being approached.
stories.ripple_effects: If the talks lead to actual agreements (on model release rules, chip controls, or research safety standards), it could ease the current cycle of tit-for-tat tech restrictions that have been rattling chipmakers, cloud companies and AI labs on both sides. If they fail or collapse, it could instead harden the current split into two separate, incompatible AI ecosystems — one US-led, one China-led — which every other country, including India, will eventually have to choose between or navigate.
stories.why_now: The talks are a direct follow-through on commitments made at the Trump-Xi summit in May, and are being scheduled deliberately ahead of Xi's September 24 US visit so both sides can show progress before that higher-profile meeting.
stories.watch_next: Whether the September talks produce any concrete framework (e.g. on frontier-model release rules) or remain just a dialogue with no binding outcome.

key_terms.definition: The most advanced, capable AI systems that exist at any given time — the ones furthest ahead of what's publicly available, and the ones governments worry most about controlling.
An AI model whose underlying parameters are published so anyone can download and run it, as opposed to a model only accessible through a paid API.

stories.the_lesson: When a new technology becomes powerful enough to threaten national security on its own, rival governments eventually move from purely restricting each other to negotiating directly — the same pattern nuclear weapons followed last century.
stories.market_link: AI/semiconductor stocks (Nvidia, AMD, Chinese chipmakers) and US-listed Chinese tech names are sensitive to any signal of eased or tightened export controls coming out of these talks.

stories.title: Trump threatens to bomb an Iranian bridge or power plant every time Iran hits a ship in the Strait of Hormuz

thread.previously: We told you the US and Iran had moved from a stand-off to direct combat: three US troops were killed, and Washington struck Iranian targets to try to force the Strait of Hormuz back open to oil traffic.
thread.changed: Trump has now issued a standing threat: every time Iran fires on a ship in the Strait, the US will destroy one bridge or power plant in Iran, including near Tehran. A US strike already hit the Iranian island of Larak in the Strait, and Iran's government says over 50 people have been killed by US strikes in the past month. Iran has vowed to hit 'infrastructure and bridges across the region' in response, and shipping through the Strait has dropped to a three-week low as tankers avoid the risk.

key_points: Trump: one bridge or power plant destroyed for every Iranian shot at a ship
A US strike already hit Iran's Larak island in the Strait
Iran vows to hit regional 'infrastructure and bridges' back
Tanker traffic through the Strait falls to a 3-week low
stories.background: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow sea channel between Iran and the Arabian Peninsula that roughly a fifth of the world's oil and a big share of its gas passes through every day — there's no practical way around it. Iran has effectively been at war with the US and its allies over control of this chokepoint for weeks: Washington wants it kept open for global shipping, Iran has been attacking vessels to pressure the US and Israel to back off. Three American troops were killed and the US has already bombed Iranian targets once to try to reopen the route.
stories.what_happened: President Trump posted a public warning that from now on, any time Iran shoots at a ship in the Strait of Hormuz — with a missile, rocket, drone or anything else — the US will respond by destroying one bridge or power plant inside Iran, including ones near the capital, Tehran. A US strike has already hit the small Iranian island of Larak, which sits in the Strait. Iran's health ministry says US strikes have killed more than 50 people and injured nearly 600 over the past month. A senior Iranian official responded that Tehran would strike 'infrastructure and bridges across the region' if the US follows through.
stories.why_it_matters: This turns a case-by-case military response into an automatic, public trigger — meaning the next Iranian attack on a ship doesn't just risk retaliation, it guarantees a specific one, on Iranian civilian infrastructure like power grids that ordinary people depend on. That raises the odds of a strike that kills civilians and pulls the conflict further from 'targeted military response' toward something that looks like open war.
stories.ripple_effects: Fewer tankers are willing to risk the Strait, which means oil and gas that would normally flow through it either doesn't move or takes a longer, costlier route — pushing up energy prices globally, including at the pump. Every country that imports oil through Hormuz (including India, a major buyer of Gulf crude) is exposed. Congress is also being asked for billions more to fund the campaign, which the Pentagon says has already cost $37.5 billion — a bill that lands on US taxpayers regardless of how the war ends.
stories.why_now: Trump's threat is a direct response to the pattern of Iranian attacks on shipping continuing despite US strikes — he's trying to change Iran's incentives by making the cost of any future attack explicit and immediate.
stories.watch_next: Whether Iran actually strikes another ship in the Strait — which would trigger Trump's promised bombing of a named bridge or power plant, a major escalation.

key_terms.definition: A narrow sea passage between Iran and Oman that a large share of the world's traded oil and gas must pass through — there is no easy alternate route.

stories.the_lesson: When a threat becomes automatic and public ('every time X, we do Y'), it removes the room for de-escalation that quiet, case-by-case retaliation usually preserves — both sides lose the ability to back down without losing face.
stories.market_link: Oil prices (Brent/WTI) and shipping/tanker stocks move directly on Strait of Hormuz risk; India's oil-import-heavy economy and the rupee are exposed given India's reliance on Gulf crude.
